summary: I was born in Karen State, Burma, in the year 2000. Karen State is where my family and I originally come from before we became refugees. It was a place that shaped my early life before we had to leave due to difficult circumstances. My connection to Karen State is deep because it is my homeland and where my story began.

Burma, also known as Myanmar, is the country where Karen State is located and where I was born. Life in Burma was challenging for my family, which eventually led us to flee and become refugees. The political and social conditions in Burma influenced our decision to leave and seek safety elsewhere. Burma will always be part of my identity as my birthplace.

My family and I lived in the Mae La Oo refugee camp in Thailand for many years. Life there was hard, especially for my parents who struggled to find jobs. The camp was a place of refuge but also of many challenges, like limited food and opportunities. It was where I grew up before we resettled in the United States in 2011.

Thailand was the country where my family took refuge after leaving Burma. We lived in the Mae La Oo refugee camp there, and my dad tried to find work outside the camp but faced difficulties, including being jailed for a month. We traveled through places like Mae Sot and Bangkok in Thailand during our journey to the United States. Thailand was both a place of refuge and struggle for us.

In 2011, my family resettled in the United States, which was a big change for us. At first, it was hard because we didn't understand English well and had to adjust to a new culture and environment. My dad found a job in a shampoo company but faced unfair treatment. Despite the challenges, I now feel safe and comfortable living in the U.S., and it has become my new home.

After arriving in the United States, my family settled in St. Paul, Minnesota. This is where I live now and where I attend school. Living in St. Paul has been a big adjustment from my life in the refugee camp and Thailand, but I feel safe here. The city is where my family is building a new life.

I am currently a student at Washington Technology Magnet School in St. Paul. Going to this school is part of my journey to improve my English and adapt to life in the United States. School has been important for me to learn and grow after coming from a refugee camp. It represents a new opportunity for my future.

Mae Sot is a town in Thailand that we traveled through on our way from the refugee camp to the United States. It was part of the long and worrying journey my family took in 2011. I remember being scared and seeing my younger brother faint in the car because of the smell on the dirt road there. Mae Sot was a difficult but important stop on our path to a new life.

In Bangkok, Thailand, we went through airport security on our way to the United States. I didn't understand why the security took away our belongings, and I thought they might arrive in the U.S. before us. The airport experience was confusing and stressful for me as a child. Bangkok was a key point in our journey to America.

During our travel to the United States, we stopped in Hong Kong where I got lost in the airport for one or two hours. My mom was very worried and cried because she couldn't find me. This was a scary moment for me, but we eventually reunited and continued our journey. Hong Kong was an unexpected challenge on our way to a new life.
